content: Knowing your two core values by name is not an abstract exercise — it is the operational foundation of every courageous decision. Brown's research found that most people cannot name their values with precision, which means they are making consequential choices without a compass. When you cannot articulate what you actually stand for, you default to what is comfortable, expedient, or socially approved rather than what is right.
implication: Leaders and founders who cannot name their two core values are effectively rudderless under pressure. Identifying and committing to exactly two — not ten — forces the kind of specificity that makes values actionable rather than decorative.

content: Brown's research on values-driven leadership identified a consistent pattern: people who live in accordance with their values report greater courage, more resilience, and less burnout than those who do not. Values are not aspirational posters on the wall — they are the specific behaviors, practices, and boundaries you are willing to enact even when it costs you something. A value you are unwilling to be inconvenienced by is not actually a value; it is a preference.
implication: Organizations that list values but tolerate behaviors that violate them do not have a values problem — they have a courage problem. The audit question is not 'what are our values?' but 'what did we actually do last quarter that proves it?'

content: Living with integrity, in Brown's framing, means choosing courage over comfort and choosing what is right over what is fun, fast, or easy. Integrity is not a fixed character trait people either have or lack — it is a practice, a daily series of choices under conditions of uncertainty and social pressure. The gap between who we say we are and how we actually behave in hard moments is where integrity is either built or eroded.
implication: For builders and leaders, integrity shows up most visibly in the small, unglamorous decisions made under pressure — not in mission statements. Closing the gap between stated values and daily behavior is the actual work of culture-building.

content: Brown makes a sharp distinction between values as nouns and values as verbs. Courage, creativity, and integrity sound like admirable traits to possess, but they only become real through specific, observable behaviors enacted under pressure. The exercise she uses with leaders is to ask: if your values are courage and connection, name five behaviors you do consistently that prove it — and five you would have to stop doing to honor them.
implication: Values operationalization is the missing step in most leadership development. Translating abstract values into concrete, auditable behaviors is what separates organizations that live their values from those that merely list them.

content: One of the most consistent findings in Brown's research on daring leaders is that they have a clear sense of their own values and are willing to hold the boundary between what is acceptable and what is not, even when it is costly. Leaders who are unclear on their values are the most susceptible to ethical drift — the slow, incremental erosion of standards that happens when each individual compromise seems minor in isolation.
implication: Ethical drift is not usually a dramatic moment of corruption — it is a long series of small compromises made by people who could not name what they were unwilling to trade away. Values clarity is the first line of defense against it.

content: Brown argues that practicing your values sometimes means disappointing people — and that the discomfort of disappointing others is one of the clearest signals that you are actually living your values rather than performing them. When staying true to your values is always convenient and socially rewarded, you have no way of knowing whether you actually hold them or merely prefer them when they are cost-free.
implication: For leaders, the willingness to disappoint — a board, a customer, a colleague, even yourself — in service of a value you have named and committed to is one of the most reliable measures of integrity. Painless values are almost certainly performative.
source_ref: Brené Brown on How to Lead With Vulnerability at Work | The Interview

content: In her leadership research, Brown found that many people confuse values with aspirations — they name the person they want to be rather than identifying the beliefs that already guide their actual behavior. The exercise of identifying your real values requires looking backward at the moments you were proudest, the decisions that cost you something but still felt right, and the violations that produced genuine moral pain — not forward at an idealized self.
implication: Values discovery is forensic, not aspirational. Before building a team culture around stated values, leaders should examine what the organization's behavior already proves it values — then decide whether that is the culture they want to defend or reform.

content: Brown identifies self-righteousness as a corruption of values-driven living that she calls a significant risk. When values become identity armor rather than a compass, people use them to judge and exclude rather than to guide their own behavior. The signal that values have curdled into self-righteousness is when you are more focused on whether others are living up to them than on whether you are.
implication: Values-driven culture can become moralistic culture if leaders use values language primarily to police others rather than to hold themselves accountable. The healthiest values cultures are characterized by personal rigor and relational generosity, not public performance of virtue.

content: Brown is explicit that integrity is not about being perfect or never making mistakes — it is about having a reliable mechanism for noticing when your behavior has drifted from your values and returning without excessive self-punishment. The practice of integrity includes the practice of self-compassion, because shame-based self-criticism after a values failure tends to produce more of the same behavior rather than genuine course correction.
implication: Building an integrity culture requires normalizing the process of noticing drift and repairing it — not creating an environment where mistakes are so costly to admit that people hide them. Leaders who model self-compassionate accountability unlock the same capacity in their teams.

content: Brown's work consistently connects values clarity with the ability to set and hold boundaries. When you know your two core values with precision, you have a principled basis for saying no that does not require elaborate justification or social negotiation. Without that clarity, boundaries feel arbitrary or aggressive, and people either abandon them under pressure or enforce them with hostility because they cannot articulate why the line matters.
implication: Teaching teams to name their values before teaching them to set boundaries reverses a common failure in boundary-setting training. Values give boundaries meaning — without the underlying why, a no is just a refusal rather than an act of integrity.
source_ref: Brené Brown on Boundaries, Feelings & Core Emotions | Ten Percent Happier Podcast with Dan Harris

content: Privilege and values integrity are directly connected in Brown's framework: remaining silent when your values call for you to speak is a form of values betrayal, regardless of how uncomfortable speaking up might be. She addresses this particularly in the context of privilege — noting that the people best positioned to challenge injustice are often the ones who have the most to lose socially by doing so, which is precisely why it requires courage grounded in values rather than comfort.
implication: For leaders in positions of privilege, staying silent in the face of injustice is never a neutral act — it is a choice that has measurable consequences for the people who depend on their voice. Values integrity in leadership requires speaking up proportional to your power, not your comfort.
source_ref: What Is Privilege: Brené Brown On Staying Silent vs. Speaking Up

content: Brown describes the experience of living out of alignment with your values as a specific kind of inner turbulence — a background sense of unease, inauthenticity, or low-grade resentment that accumulates over time. People often mistake this signal for anxiety or dissatisfaction with external circumstances, when in fact it is the felt experience of a values violation that has not been named or addressed. Identifying the violation and taking action to correct it almost always produces immediate, measurable relief.
implication: Chronic, diffuse dissatisfaction at work is often a values misalignment signal, not a workload or compensation problem. Leaders who investigate persistent team disengagement through a values-alignment lens will frequently discover more useful information than through a satisfaction survey.

content: Brown's concept of 'integrity as practice' extends to the language we use with ourselves and others. Saying something is 'fine' when it is not, agreeing to something you do not intend to honor, or offering validation you do not genuinely feel are all small integrity violations that accumulate into a pattern of inauthenticity. The compounding effect of these small dishonesties is a progressive disconnection from your own inner life and from the people around you.
implication: For teams trying to build psychological safety, the smallest integrity failures — the performative agreement, the withheld concern, the fake 'sounds great' — corrode trust faster than large, visible failures because they happen constantly and invisibly. Naming and interrupting this pattern requires values clarity and the courage to be uncomfortable.

content: Brown draws a direct line between values-grounded living and the capacity for joy. In her interviews with people she describes as wholehearted, she found that those who could name their values and act on them consistently reported significantly higher levels of gratitude and satisfaction than those who felt internally fragmented or chronically compromised. Values clarity is not merely an ethical matter — it is a prerequisite for psychological wholeness.
implication: The business case for helping people identify and live their values is not only ethical — it is directly tied to engagement, retention, and creative output. People who feel internally coherent bring more of themselves to their work; people who feel morally compromised disengage or leave.
source_ref: Brené Brown - Living With a Whole Heart | Super Soul Sunday S4E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown makes a pointed observation that values integrity is hardest to maintain not in moments of dramatic moral choice but in the ordinary, accumulating decisions made under time pressure, social influence, and competing obligations. The most dangerous threat to integrity is not a sudden temptation but a gradual drift enabled by busyness, avoidance, and the human desire to be liked and approved of.
implication: The greatest integrity risk in high-growth organizations is not a single bad actor — it is the slow normalization of expedient compromises made by well-meaning people moving too fast to notice what they are trading away. Slowing down to ask 'does this reflect what we actually stand for?' is a structural practice, not a luxury.

content: Brown's research found that people who live with the most integrity are not those who never struggle with temptation or ambiguity — they are those who have developed a reliable practice of returning to their values when they notice they have drifted. The returning, not the perfection, is the practice. This reframe makes integrity genuinely accessible rather than a standard reserved for morally exceptional individuals.
implication: Leaders who model the practice of noticing drift and returning to values — openly, without excessive self-flagellation — give their teams permission to do the same. Cultures of integrity are built by the quality of the repair, not by the absence of failure.

content: Brown explicitly addresses the relationship between values and the tendency to people-please, noting that chronic approval-seeking is incompatible with values-driven living. When your primary goal is to be liked, you will inevitably compromise what you believe in order to gain or maintain approval. The antidote is not indifference to others but a sufficiently grounded sense of self that you can tolerate disapproval without experiencing it as a threat to your worth.
implication: People-pleasing is not a personality quirk — it is a structural values problem. Leaders who build their sense of safety on others' approval will consistently make decisions that protect relationships at the expense of integrity. The first step out is naming and anchoring to a value more important than being liked.
source_ref: The Real Reason You Struggle with Self-Worth - And How to Change It | Professor Brené Brown

content: In Brown's framework, the act of naming your values publicly — to your team, your family, or your community — creates a form of accountability that makes values-consistent living both harder to abandon and more meaningful to enact. Privately held values are fragile; spoken values become a kind of social contract with the people whose respect you have chosen to risk. That vulnerability is not performative — it is the mechanism by which values develop behavioral weight.
implication: Leaders who name their values explicitly and publicly are more accountable to them than those who hold them privately. Public naming is not virtue signaling when it is followed by observable behavior — it is the courageous act that closes the loop between aspiration and action.

content: Brown is clear that choosing comfort over values is always a form of compromise, even when the choice feels entirely reasonable in the moment. The comfort she identifies as most seductive is not laziness but the comfort of avoiding difficult conversations, withholding hard truths, and tolerating behavior that violates your values because addressing it would be uncomfortable. Every time you choose that comfort, you make the next choice slightly harder.
implication: The compounding cost of avoided hard conversations is one of the least-visible destroyers of organizational health. Each deferred confrontation raises the emotional cost of the next one, until the accumulated avoidance has created a culture where nothing difficult can be said by anyone.

content: Brown connects the inability to identify one's values with what she describes as feeling lost — a pervasive sense of disconnection from purpose and direction that she documented frequently in her research participants. The experience of feeling lost is rarely about external circumstances; it is almost always about an internal incoherence between how you are living and what you actually believe. Naming your values is frequently the first and most important step toward feeling found.
implication: When team members report feeling disengaged, purposeless, or burnt out, the diagnostic question is often less about workload and more about values alignment. Helping people reconnect to what they stand for — and creating conditions where they can act on it — is one of the highest-leverage investments a leader can make.
source_ref: This Is Why You FEEL LOST, LAZY & UNMOTIVATED In Life... | Brené Brown

content: Brown's research consistently shows that the most courageous acts people describe are not physically dangerous but values-threatening — speaking a truth that could damage a relationship, making a decision that could cost you professionally, or refusing to participate in something that conflicts with what you believe. The arena, in her framing, is the everyday space where values meet social pressure, and showing up in that space with integrity intact is the definition of daring greatly.
implication: Courage in organizational life is almost never about physical bravery — it is about values-grounded action under social and professional risk. Recognizing and honoring that form of courage in others is how leaders build the kind of culture where people bring their full integrity to their work.
source_ref: Brené Brown: Why Your Critics Aren't The Ones Who Count

content: Brown has noted that the values most commonly chosen by leaders in her research are often the values they perform rather than the ones that guide their actual decisions under stress. Under pressure, comfort, approval, and fear override stated values almost automatically unless those values have been explicitly practiced through the repeated experience of choosing them when it costs something. Values, like muscles, only develop through resistance.
implication: Values training that consists only of identification and articulation — without practice under conditions of social or professional resistance — produces cosmetic culture change. Leaders must create deliberate situations where people practice choosing their values at a small cost to build the capacity to choose them at a large one.

content: Brown frames living with integrity as an act of self-respect that has nothing to do with perfectionism. Perfectionism is a defense mechanism designed to earn approval; integrity is a commitment to yourself that exists independent of external judgment. This distinction matters because perfectionist behavior often masquerades as high standards or strong values, while actually being driven by shame and fear of being found inadequate.
implication: Leaders who conflate perfectionism with integrity will create environments of unrelenting self-criticism and punishing performance cultures. Distinguishing between the two — and modeling the difference — is essential for building teams where people can take the risks that actual integrity requires.

content: Brown describes the experience of acting against your values as a specific form of moral injury — distinct from ordinary mistake-making — that produces a particular quality of shame and self-alienation. When you do something you know to be wrong, or fail to do something you know to be required, the injury is not to your reputation but to your relationship with yourself. Repairing that relationship requires acknowledgment, accountability, and recommitment — not merely apology to others.
implication: Leaders who treat integrity failures purely as reputational problems to be managed will miss the internal repair work that actually restores integrity. The most important audience for any integrity repair is not external stakeholders but your own sense of self — and the repair requires honest reckoning, not PR strategy.
